From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the current political party in Brazil. For the historic political party with the same name, see [Social Democratic Party (Brazil, 1945–65)](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(Brazil,_1945%E2%80%9365) "Social Democratic Party (Brazil, 1945–65)"). For other parties with similar names, see [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party "Social Democratic Party").

The **Social Democratic Party** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Partido Social Democrático*, **PSD**) is a [political party in Brazil](/wiki/Political_party_in_Brazil "Political party in Brazil") led by [Gilberto Kassab](/wiki/Gilberto_Kassab "Gilberto Kassab") and uniting dissidents from various political parties, especially the [Democrats](/wiki/Democrats_(Brazil) "Democrats (Brazil)"), [Brazilian Social Democracy Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Social_Democracy_Party "Brazilian Social Democracy Party") and [Party of National Mobilization](/wiki/Party_of_National_Mobilization "Party of National Mobilization").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The party has become a major force for [centrism in Brazil](/wiki/Centrism_in_Brazil "Centrism in Brazil") and commonly unites with both left-wing and right-wing parties. In this way, the party managed to have members occupying positions in ministries and important secretariats in the governments of presidents [Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Dilma_Rousseff "Dilma Rousseff"), [Michel Temer](/wiki/Michel_Temer "Michel Temer"), and [Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva](/wiki/Luiz_In%C3%A1cio_Lula_da_Silva "Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva"). As such, PSD has been considered by political scientists as a [big tent](/wiki/Big_tent "Big tent") party[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) and part of the *[Centrão](/wiki/Centr%C3%A3o "Centrão")*.[[16]](#cite_note-16) While one of the youngest parties in Brazil, PSD's pragmatic nature has allowed it to grow exponentially, becoming one of the largest parties in Brazil.

After the [2024 Brazilian municipal elections](/wiki/2024_Brazilian_municipal_elections "2024 Brazilian municipal elections"), it has become the party with the most number of mayors in Brazil.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_Democratic_Party_(Brazil,_2011)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The party was founded in 2011 by [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") mayor [Gilberto Kassab](/wiki/Gilberto_Kassab "Gilberto Kassab"), bringing together dissidents from Kassab’s former party, the [Democrats](/wiki/Democrats_(Brazil) "Democrats (Brazil)") (DEM), and from the [Brazilian Social Democracy Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Social_Democracy_Party "Brazilian Social Democracy Party") (PSDB), as well as some members from the [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressistas "Progressistas") (PP), the [Brazilian Labour Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Labour_Party_(1981) "Brazilian Labour Party (1981)") (PTB), and others. It united politicians who opposed their parties’ “automatic opposition” to the [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)") (PT) and advocated for a more pragmatic stance, open to dialogue with the government, while positioning itself as an alternative to the [Brazilian Democratic Movement Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Democratic_Movement_Party "Brazilian Democratic Movement Party") (PMDB), as the traditional force in [big tent](/wiki/Big_tent "Big tent") politics.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The foundation was criticized by members of the opposition political parties, primarily the Democrats (DEM), as supporting the government. Many Democrats members accused the PSD of being created by Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva to destroy the DEM and deliver on a promise made to [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)") (PT) supporters to "exterminate the DEM from the Brazilian political scenario".[[19]](#cite_note-19) The party was criticized for having a vague program, making references to [economic freedom](/wiki/Economic_freedom "Economic freedom"), political reform and welfare, and being hard to differentiate from other political parties except those on the [hard left](/wiki/Hard_left "Hard left"). According to Kassab, "We are neither a right-wing party nor a left-wing nor a centrist party".[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In many Brazilian states, the PSD took a [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") stance on economics and had good election results. The party has good relationships with major political parties such as the [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") [Brazilian Socialist Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Socialist_Party "Brazilian Socialist Party"), the [national conservative](/wiki/National_conservative "National conservative") [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressistas "Progressistas"), the [centrist](/wiki/Centrist "Centrist") [Brazilian Labour Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Labour_Party_(current) "Brazilian Labour Party (current)"), the [Christian democratic](/wiki/Christian_democracy "Christian democracy") [Brazilian Republican Party](/wiki/Brazilian_Republican_Party "Brazilian Republican Party") and the [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism") [Citizenship](/wiki/Cidadania "Cidadania"). Since its inception, the party has been associated with fusions with other parties, primarily the PP and PSB. It has endured on the Brazilian political scene, and has more representation than any political party other than the major ones. The party chaired the [Finance Ministry of Brazil](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Economy_(Brazil) "Ministry of the Economy (Brazil)") in Michel Temer's government, and is a major force in Brazilian politics.

However, in the [state of Bahia](/wiki/State_of_Bahia "State of Bahia"), the party is a long-term partner of the major left-wing [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)"), having the running mate in the gubernatorial tickets of Rui Costa and each party supporting the other in the senate elections, with [Otto Alencar](/wiki/Otto_Alencar "Otto Alencar") and [Angelo Coronel](/wiki/Angelo_Coronel "Angelo Coronel") being the senators of Bahia together with the *[Petista](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)")* [Jaques Wagner](/wiki/Jaques_Wagner "Jaques Wagner").

The PSD supported the [impeachment of Dilma Rousseff](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Dilma_Rousseff "Impeachment of Dilma Rousseff").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

PSD elected several senators and some governors in the Brazilian [Centro-Sul](/wiki/Centro-Sul "Centro-Sul") region in 2018, with the support of [Jair Bolsonaro](/wiki/Jair_Bolsonaro "Jair Bolsonaro").

## Ideology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_Democratic_Party_(Brazil,_2011)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ideology")]

Soon after its foundation, the national president of the party, Gilberto Kassab (SP), stated: "It will not be on the right, it will not be on the left, nor on the center". The foundational purpose of the PSD is the satisfaction of the interests of the lower class that had risen to the middle class position during the Workers' Party governments.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The then-vice-president of the party, [Guilherme Affif Domingos](/wiki/Guilherme_Afif_Domingos "Guilherme Afif Domingos") (SP) wrote a document with 12 party commitments, among them:

1. National Development
2. Liberty
3. Democracy and Regional Representation
4. The Right to Security of Property
5. Equal Opportunities
6. Sustainability and Technological Innovation
7. Accountability and [Fiscal Responsibility](/wiki/Balanced_budget "Balanced budget")
8. [Freedom of the Press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press")
9. Free Association
10. [Decentralization](/wiki/Decentralization "Decentralization") and Federalism
11. Free Trade and Defense of Traditional Values
12. Individual Freedom and Responsibility[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

However, according to political scientist Rui Maluf, the absence of specific programmatic content reveals that the PSD has a typically *catch-all* organizational nature and that its foundation reflects the dissatisfaction of its staff with previous parties, mostly from the [Democratas](/wiki/Democrats_(Brazil) "Democrats (Brazil)") party.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Notable members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_Democratic_Party_(Brazil,_2011)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable members")]

* [Gilberto Kassab](/wiki/Gilberto_Kassab "Gilberto Kassab"), former mayor of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") and Former [Minister of Science, Technology, Innovation and Communications](/wiki/Ministry_of_Science,_Technology,_Innovation_and_Communications_(Brazil) "Ministry of Science, Technology, Innovation and Communications (Brazil)")
* [Ana Amelia Lemos](/wiki/Ana_Am%C3%A9lia_Lemos "Ana Amélia Lemos"), journalist, former senator of Republic for [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"), and vice-presidential candidate for [Geraldo Alckmin](/wiki/Geraldo_Alckmin "Geraldo Alckmin") in 2018
* [Rodrigo Pacheco](/wiki/Rodrigo_Pacheco_(politician) "Rodrigo Pacheco (politician)"), Senator of Republic for Minas Gerais and President of the Federal Senate and National Congress
* [Mara Gabrilli](/wiki/Mara_Gabrilli "Mara Gabrilli"), Senator of Republic for [Sao Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), and vice-presidential candidate for [Simone Tebet](/wiki/Simone_Tebet "Simone Tebet") in 2022
* [Hassan de Zé Cocá](/wiki/Hassan_de_Z%C3%A9_Coc%C3%A1 "Hassan de Zé Cocá"), State deputy from Bahia
* [Eduardo Leite](/wiki/Eduardo_Leite "Eduardo Leite"), [Governor of Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Governor_of_Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Governor of Rio Grande do Sul")
* [Marcelo Calero](/wiki/Marcelo_Calero "Marcelo Calero"), former [Minister of Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_(Brazil) "Ministry of Culture (Brazil)")
